summary: Gruber critiques a Dateline NBC report that argues Apple should track stolen iPods using registration data. He points out the practical and privacy complications of tracking 110 million devices, notes Dateline's misleading framing that competitors don't do 'anything more' than Apple (meaning they do nothing at all), and flags the irony of Dateline praising an Apple patent that could prevent competitors from implementing similar theft-deterrence features.
key_points: Dateline's suggestion that Apple track all 110 million iPods ignores both the enormous engineering effort and the serious privacy implications
Dateline's phrasing that competitors don't do 'anything more than Apple' cleverly obscures the fact that no MP3 player maker tracks stolen devices at all
Apple's patent application for theft-deterrent charging could be used to prevent competitors from implementing similar features, undermining the very goal Dateline celebrates
The ex-partner scenario illustrates how a stolen-device tracking system would immediately become a privacy and harassment nightmare
money_quotes: Would it be possible for Apple to do something about this? Yes. Easy or simple? No.
How would it not be a privacy nightmare? Imagine all the iPods given to ex-boyfriends / ex-girlfriends / ex-spouses that would be reported as "stolen".
By which they mean that none of Apple's competitors in the MP3 player market do anything to track stolen players.

Dateline's approval of which is curious — or at least shortsighted — given that, if approved, Apple could use such a patent to prevent any other gadget maker from implementing theft-deterring device tracking.
key_insight: Dateline's report on iPod theft inadvertently highlights both the media's tendency to demand simple solutions to complex problems and the perverse incentives of software patents.
